checks.a_analysis: I have compared the output JSON text against the source PDF. All paragraphs, list items, and headings in the output correspond to content present in the source document. There are no fabricated sections or hallucinated text.

The source document contains no phone numbers, email addresses, or URLs. The output correctly reflects this and does not introduce any fabricated contact information. Mentions of companies (AT&T, RapidDeploy, ECaTS) and locations (Dallas, TX) are accurate to the source.

All four pages of the source PDF are accounted for in the output JSON. The header information, attendance table, all 14 agenda items, and the various vote tables are all present in the converted document.

I counted the rows in all tables in the source and output. The attendance table contains all members and staff listed. The three vote tables (Items 4, 5, and 6) each contain 10 member rows, matching the source document perfectly.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The text content of each agenda item and the goals listed under item 6 have been reviewed. All substantial text, including specific project updates, names of individuals, dates, and financial figures, has been accurately preserved in the output. The absence of the diagonal 'Approved 2/10/2022' watermark is considered an acceptable exclusion of non-flow background text.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the output follows the source document very closely, including specific spellings (e.g., 'Glen Knox' vs 'Glenn Knox' as they appear in the source) and punctuation. No meaning-altering changes were found.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the source PDF and the converted JSON. The document contains several tables with identical lists of members (Rachel Bello, Randy Cress, etc.), but this is a faithful representation of the source material which records votes for different items using the same list. The logo is repeated at the top of each page in the source, which is reflected by the presence of multiple image elements in the JSON. No unintended duplication was found.

The heading hierarchy begins with H2 for the document title and main sections, followed by H3 for sub-sections (e.g., 'Minutes Vote'). While starting with H2 instead of H1 is slightly non-standard, it is consistent and does not skip levels. No structural breaks or incorrect nesting were identified.

The table structures generally follow the source. However, in three of the tables ('Minutes Vote', '2022 Technology Committee Dates Vote', and '2022 Technology Committee Goals Vote'), the first data row (containing the vote for 'Rachel Bello') is incorrectly identified as a header row (`is_header_row: true`). These tables in the source do not have header rows; they are simple two-column lists of names and votes.
checks.b_evidence_source: The tables 'Minutes Vote' (page 2), '2022 Technology Committee Dates Vote' (page 2), and Goal Vote (page 3) consist of two columns without explicit header labels.
checks.b_evidence_output: The tables at element index 9, 11, and 15 all have "is_header_row": true for the first row of data.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order in the JSON follows the logical top-to-bottom flow of the source PDF, including the placement of tables and paragraphs relative to the numbered list items.

The source document contains a single continuous numbered list (1 through 14) interspersed with tables and text. The conversion has split this into five separate ordered list objects. Because these are separate objects, the numbering resets to 1 for each block (e.g., item 5 is the first item in its list, item 6 is the first in its list, and items 7-9 become 1-3). Furthermore, the original numbering (e.g., '5.', '6.', '7.') has been stripped from the text content, meaning the original sequence numbering is lost in the output.
checks.b_evidence_source: Continuous numbered list from 1 to 14 across 4 pages.
checks.b_evidence_output: Multiple separate list elements (e.g., element index 10, 12, 16, 18) where numbering resets to 1 and the original numeric prefixes are removed.

checks.a_analysis: There are no links in the source document, and none have been incorrectly injected into the JSON output.

checks.a_analysis: The visual organization of the document is significantly altered in the HTML version. First, the continuous numbered list from the source PDF (items 1 through 14) is fragmented into multiple separate lists in the HTML. Each time a new 'page' section begins (marked by the DIT logo), the list numbering restarts at 1. For example, item 5, item 6, and item 10 from the source PDF are all labeled as '1.' in the HTML. Second, the table '2022 Technology Committee Goals Vote', which follows item 6 in the source PDF (page 3), has been moved to the end of the section in the HTML, appearing after item 9. This disrupts the logical flow and relationship between the text and the corresponding vote data.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF shows a continuous list numbering (1-14) across pages 2-4. On page 3, item 6 is immediately followed by its corresponding vote table.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows numbering restarting: '1. 2022 Technology Committee Dates Vote' (originally 5), '1. 2022 Technology Committee Goals' (originally 6), and '1. NMAC' (originally 10). The '2022 Technology Committee Goals Vote' table is positioned after item 3 (originally 9: 'Business Intelligence Invitation for Bid').
checks.b_evidence_location: Items 5, 6, and 10; Table '2022 Technology Committee Goals Vote'.

checks.a_analysis: All tables found in the source PDF (Attendance, Minutes Vote, Dates Vote, and Goals Vote) are correctly rendered in the HTML. They maintain visible borders, clear columns, and readable data. Headers are visually distinct.

The primary meaningful image, the 'NC DIT 911 Board' logo, is present in the HTML output, appearing at the start of sections corresponding to the original PDF pages. The red 'Approved' watermark is missing from the HTML, but this is an acceptable and intentional accessibility improvement for decorative elements.

There is no evidence of body content duplication (paragraphs or data tables) in the HTML output. While the logo and certain header text repeat, this is a result of converting per-page headers from the source PDF and is considered standard behavior rather than a defect.
